name: Raven
description: Raven is a central figure in the mythology of many Pacific Northwest Coast peoples, including the Haida, Tlingit, Tsimshian, and others. He is a complex and often contradictory figure, embodying characteristics of both a creator and a trickster. Raven is often credited with bringing light to the world, creating the first humans, and releasing the sun, moon, and stars from containers where they were held captive. He achieves these feats through cunning, deception, and sometimes, sheer luck. Raven's motivations are often selfish or driven by curiosity, leading him to both benevolent and destructive actions. He is a powerful shapeshifter, capable of transforming into various animals and humans. He is revered as a cultural hero but also feared for his unpredictable nature and his ability to disrupt the established order. His stories often serve as moral lessons, highlighting the importance of balance and the consequences of greed and hubris. He represents the interconnectedness of the natural world and the ever-present potential for change and transformation. Raven is not a god in the traditional sense, but rather a powerful being who helped shape the world and continues to influence it through his descendants and the stories told about him. He is a symbol of intelligence, adaptability, and the enduring power of myth.
subtitle: Creator, Trickster, Transformer, and Thief

keyMyths.title: Raven Steals the Sun
keyMyths.description: Raven transforms into a hemlock needle and is swallowed by the daughter of the Sky Chief. He is reborn and eventually steals the sun, moon, and stars from her father, bringing light to the world.

keyMyths.title: Raven Creates the First Humans
keyMyths.description: Raven finds the first humans hidden in a clamshell (or other container, depending on the tribe) and releases them into the world, teaching them how to survive.

keyMyths.title: Raven Steals Fire
keyMyths.description: Raven steals fire from the Sky Chief (or another being, depending on the tribe) and brings it to humanity, allowing them to cook food and keep warm.

sources.author: Franz Boas
